By Theorem 6.14, (α ˇf + βˇg) : I → R is Riemann integrable, and Z I (α ˇf + βˇg) = α Z I ˇf + β Z I ˇg = α Z D f + β Z D g. It follows that (αf + βg) : D → R is also Riemann integrable, and Z D (αf + βg) = Z I (α ˇf + βˇg) = α Z D f + β Z D g.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 386 Theorem 6.23 Let D be a bounded subset of Rn, and let f : D → R be a bounded function defined on D. Assume that f(x) ≥ 0 for all x in D. If f : D → R is Riemann integrable, then Z D f ≥ 0. Proof Let I = nY i=1 [ai, bi] be a closed rectangle that contains D, and let ˇf : I → R be the zero extension of f : D → R to I. Since f : D → R is Riemann integrable, ˇf : I → R is also Riemann integrable. It is easy to check that ˇf(x) ≥ 0 for all x in I. Therefore, Z D f = Z I ˇf ≥ 0. As before, monotonicity is a consequence of linearity and Theorem 6.23. Theorem 6.24 Monotonicity Let D be a bounded subset of Rn, and let f : D → R and g : D → R be Riemann integrable functions. If f(x) ≥ g(x) for all x in D, then Z D f ≥ Z D g. At the end of this section, we want to present two theorems whose proofs are almost verbatim those for the n = 1 case. The first theorem says that if a function is Riemann integrable, so is its absolute value. Theorem 6.25 Absolute Value of Riemann Integrable Functions Let D be a bounded subset ofRn, and let f : D → R be a bounded function defined on D. If the function f : D → R is Riemann integrable, then the function |f | : D → R is also Riemann integrable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 387 Sketch of Proof If ˇf : I → R is the zero extension of f : D → R to the closed rectangle I = nY i=1 [ai, bi] that contains D, then | ˇf | : I → R is the zero extension of |f | : D → R. Hence, it is sufficient to consider the case where D is a closed rectangle of the form I = nY i=1 [ai, bi]. The proof is almost the same as the n = 1 case. The key of the proof is the fact that for any subset A of I, sup x∈A |f(x)| − inf x∈A |f(x)| ≤ sup x∈A f(x) − inf x∈A f(x). The second theorem says that products of Riemann integrable functions are Riemann integrable. Theorem 6.26 Products of Riemann Integrable Functions Let D be a bounded subset of Rn, and let f : D → R and g : D → R be bounded functions defined on D. If the functions f : D → R and g : D → R are Riemann integrable, then the function(f g) : D → R is also Riemann integrable. Sketch of Proof It is sufficient to consider the case whereD is a closed rectangle of the form I = nY i=1 [ai, bi]. The proof is almost the same as the n = 1 case. The key of the proof is the fact that if M is positive number such that |f(x)| ≤ M and |g(x)| ≤ M for all x ∈ I, then for any subset A of I, sup x∈A (f g)(x) − inf x∈A (f g)(x) ≤ M  sup x∈A f(x) − inf x∈A f(x) + sup x∈A g(x) − inf x∈A g(x)  .Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 388 Exercises 6.2 Question 1 Let I = [0 , 3] × [0, 3], and let f : I → R and g : I → R be Riemann integrable functions. Suppose that f(x, y) = g(y, x) and (3f + 2g)(x, y) = 10 for all (x, y) ∈ I, find Z I f and Z I g. Question 2 Complete the details in the proof of Theorem 6.25. Question 3 Complete the details in the proof of Theorem 6.26.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 389 6.3 Jordan Measurable Sets and Riemann Integrable Functions In this section, we will give some sufficient conditions for a bounded function f : D → R to be Riemann integrable. We start with the following theorem. Theorem 6.27 Let I = nY i=1 [ai, bi], and let f : I → R be a continuous function defined on I. Then f : I → R is Riemann integrable. Proof Since f : I → R is continuous and I is compact, f : I → R is uniformly continuous. Given ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that if u and v are points in I and ∥u − v∥ < δ, then |f(u) − f(v)| < ε vol (I) . Let P be any partition of I with |P| < δ. A rectangle J in JP is a compact set. Since f : J → R is continuous, the extreme value theorem says that there exist points uJ and vJ in J such that f(uJ) ≤ f(x) ≤ f(vJ) for all x ∈ J. Therefore, mJ = inf x∈J f(x) = f(uJ) and MJ = sup x∈J f(x) = f(vJ). Since |P| < δ, ∥uJ − vJ∥ ≤ diam J ≤ |P| < δ. Therefore, MJ − mJ = f(vJ) − f(uJ) < ε vol (I) . This implies that U(f, P) − L(f, P) = X J∈JP (MJ − mJ) vol (J) < ε vol (I) X J∈JP vol (J) = ε. Hence, f : I → R is Riemann integrable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 390 Example 6.19 Let f : [0, 1] × [0, 1] → R be the function defined as f(x, y) = sin(xy). This is a composition of the sine function and a polynomial, both of which are continuous functions. Hence, f : [0 , 1] × [0, 1] → R is a continuous function. Therefore, f : [0, 1] × [0, 1] → R is Riemann integrable. In Section 6.1, we have seen that a constant function f : I → R, f(x) = c defined on I = nY i=1 [ai, bi] is Riemann integrable and its integral is Z I f = c vol (I). Since constant functions are the simplest bounded functions, it is natural to ask whether a constant function f : D → R, f(x) = c on a bounded set D is always Riemann integrable. By linearity, it is sufficient to consider the case when c = 1. When D is a closed rectangle of the form I = nY i=1 [ai, bi], the answer is affirmative and we have Z I dx = vol (I) To consider a general set D, let us first define the characteristic function of a set. Definition 6.13 Characteristic Functions Let A be a subset of Rn. The characteristic function or indicator function of the set A is the function χA : Rn → R defined as χA(x) =    1, if x ∈ A, 0, if x /∈ A.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 391 Example 6.20 Let A = {(x, y) | x > 0}. Notice that the function χA : R2 → R is χA(x, y) =    1, if x > 0, 0, if x ≤ 0. It is continuous at (x, y) if and only if x > 0 or x < 0. Figure 6.12: The set A = {(x, y) | x > 0}. Interior, Exterior and Boundary of a Set In Chapter 1, we have seen that if A is a subset of Rn, then Rn is a disjoint union of int A, ext A and ∂A. If x0 is a point in Rn, x0 ∈ int A if and only if there is an r > 0 such that B(x0, r) ⊂ A; x0 ∈ ext A if and only if there is an r > 0 such that B(x0, r) ⊂ Rn \ A; and x0 ∈ ∂A if for every r > 0, B(x0, r) contains a point in A and a point not in A. Theorem 6.28 Let A be a subset of Rn, and let χA : Rn → R be the characteristic function of A. Then the set of discontinuities of the function χA is the set ∂A.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 392 Proof Since Rn is a disjoint union of intA, ext A and ∂A, we will show that χA is continuous on int A and ext A, and discontinuous at every point in ∂A. The sets int A and ext A are open sets, and f is equal to 1 on int A and 0 on ext A. For every x0 in int A, there is an r > 0 such that B(x0, r) ⊂ A. Therefore, for any ε > 0, if x is such that ∥x − x0∥ < r, then |f(x) − f(x0)| = 0 < ε. This shows that f is continuous at x0. Similarly, if x0 is in ext A, there is an r > 0 such that B(x0, r) ⊂ Rn \ A. The same reasoning shows that f is continuous at x0. Now consider a point x0 that is in ∂A. For any k ∈ Z+, there is a point uk ∈ A and a point vk /∈ A such that uk and vk are in the neighbourhood B(x0, 1/k) of x0. The two sequences {uk} and {vk} both converge to x0, but the sequence {f(uk)} converges to 1, the sequence {f(vk)} converges to 0. This shows that f is not continuous at x0. Figure 6.13: The characteristic function of a set A is not continuous at x0 if and only if x0 ∈ ∂A. By definition, restricted to the set A, χA : A → R is the constant function χA(A) = 1. Now we define Jordan measurable sets and its volume.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 393 Definition 6.14 Jordan Measurable Sets and Volume Let D be a bounded subset of Rn. We say that D is Jordan measurable if the constant function χD : D → R is Riemann integrable. In this case, we define the volume of D as vol (D) = Z D χD = Z D dx. Example 6.21 The closed rectangle I = nY i=1 [ai, bi] is Jordan measurable, and its volume is vol (I) = Z I dx = nY i=1 (bi − ai), as what we have defined earlier. Example 6.22 Example 6.14 says that the set D = {(x, y) | 0 ≤ y ≤ x ≤ 1} is Jordan measurable and vol (D) = 1 2. Figure 6.14: The set D = {(x, y) | 0 ≤ y ≤ x ≤ 1} is Jordan measurable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 394 One might think that all bounded subsets of Rn has volumes. This is not true. An example is given below. Example 6.23 Let I = [0, 1]n and let D = {x ∈ I | x ∈ Qn} . Notice that D is a subset of the rectangle I, and the zero extension of χD : D → R is the function χD : I → R, χD(x) =    1, if all components of x are rational, 0, otherwise, which is the Dirichlet’s function. We have seen in Example 6.13 that the function χD : I → R is not Riemann integrable. Hence, χD : D → R is not Riemann integrable. This means the set D is not Jordan measurable and so it does not have a volume. This example also shows that if B is a subset of A, and the function f : A → R is Riemann integrable, the function f : B → R is not necessary Riemann integrable. The next example says that the boundary of a rectangle has volume 0. Example 6.24 Let I = nY i=1 [ai, bi], and let D = ∂ I. Notice that D is contained in I. The zero extension of χD : D → R is the function χD : I → R which vanishes on the interior of I. By Theorem 6.19, χD : I → R is Riemann integrable and Z I χD = 0. Therefore, D = ∂ I has zero volume.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 395 Remark 6.2 Darboux Sums for a Characteristic Function Given a bounded setD that is contained in the rectangleI, if P is a partition of I, L(χD, P) is the sum of the volumes of the rectangles in P that is contained in D; while U(χD, P) is the sum of the volumes of the rectangles in P that intersect D. See Figure 6.15. Thus, for D to have volume, the two numbers L(χD, P) and U(χD, P) should get closer and closer when the partitions P gets finer. Figure 6.15: The geometric quantities represented by L(χD, P) and U(χD, P) when D is the region bounded inside the circle. Our goal is to give characterization of sets that are Jordan measurable. We will consider those that have zero volumes first. The following is a useful lemma. Lemma 6.29 Let I be a closed rectangle in Rn that contains the closed rectangles I1, . . . ,Ik. There is a partition P of I such that if J is a rectangle in the partition P, then J is either contained in an Ij for some 1 ≤ j ≤ k, or J is disjoint from the interiors of Ij for all 1 ≤ j ≤ k.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 396 Sketch of Proof We construct the partitionP = (P1, . . . , Pn) in the following way. For each 1 ≤ i ≤ n, the partition points in Pi is the set of end points of the ith-edge of I, I1, . . ., Ik. One can check that this partition satisfies the requirement. See Figure 6.16 for an illustration. Figure 6.16: A partition of the rectangle I that satisfies the conditions in Lemma 6.29. Let us introduce the definition of a cube. Definition 6.15 Cubes A rectangle of the form nY i=1 [ai, bi] such that b1 − a1 = b2 − a2 = · · · = bn − an = ℓ = 2r is called a (closed) cube with side length ℓ = 2r. The center of the cube is c = (c1, c2, . . . , cn) =  a1 + b1 2 , a2 + b2 2 , . . . , an + bn 2  . We will denote such a cube by Qc,r. There are also cubes whose edges are not parallel to the coordinate axes. In this chapter, when we say a cube, we always mean a cube defined above. Now we can give a characterization of sets with zero volume.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 397 Theorem 6.30 Let D be a bounded subset of Rn. The following are equivalent. (a) The set D is Jordan measurable and it has zero volume. (b) For any ε > 0, there are finitely many closed cubes Q1, . . . , Qk such that D ⊂ k[ j=1 Qj and kX j=1 vol (Qj) < ε. (c) For any ε > 0, there are finitely many closed rectanglesI1, . . . ,Ik such that D ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij and kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε. Proof First assume that D is a Jordan measurable set with zero volume. There is a positive number R such that the closed cube Q0,R = [−R, R]n contains the set D. Let I = Q0,R. Then the function χD : I → R is Riemann integrable and Z I χD = 0. Given m ∈ Z+, let Pm be the uniformly regular partition of I into mn rectangles. Notice that each rectangle in the partition Pm is a cube. Since lim m→∞ |Pm| = 0, we have lim m→∞ U(χD, Pm) = Z I χD = 0. Given ε > 0, there is a positive integer M such that for all m ≥ M, U(χD, Pm) < ε. Consider the partition PM. Notice that for J ∈ JPM , MJ(χD) =    1, if J ∩ D ̸= ∅, 0, if J ∩ D = ∅.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 398 Let A = {J ∈ JPM | J ∩ D ̸= ∅} . Then U(χD, PM) = X J∈A vol (J). A is a finite collection of cubess. Hence, we can named the cubes in A as Q1, . . . , Qk. By construction, D ⊂ k[ j=1 Qj and kX j=1 vol (Qj) < ε. This proves that (a) implies (b). (b) implies (c) is obvious since a cube is a rectangle. Now assume that (c) holds. Given ε > 0, (c) says that there are closed rectangles I1, . . . ,Ik such that D ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij and kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε 2 . By Lemma 6.18, for each 1 ≤ j ≤ k, there is a closed rectangle ˇIj such that Ij ⊂ intˇIj and vol (ˇIj) − vol (Ij) < ε 2k . It follows that D ⊂ k[ j=1 intˇIj and kX j=1 vol (ˇIj) < ε. Let I be a closed rectangle whose interior contains the bounded set k[ j=1 ˇIj.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 399 By Lemma 6.29, there is a partitionP of I such that each rectangle J in the partition P is either contained in an ˇIj for some 1 ≤ j ≤ k, or is disjoint from the interiors of ˇIj for all 1 ≤ j ≤ k. Let B =   J ∈ JP | J ⊂ ˇIj for some 1 ≤ j ≤ k 	 . If J /∈ B, then J ∩ intˇIj = ∅ for all 1 ≤ j ≤ k. Therefore, J ∩ D = ∅. For these J, MJ(χD) = mJ(χD) = 0. If J is in B, we use the simple estimate MJ ≤ 1. Thus, U(χD, P) = X J∈B MJvol (J) ≤ X J∈B vol (J) ≤ kX j=1 vol (ˇIj) < ε. Since L(χD, P) ≥ 0, we find that U(χD, P) − L(χD, P) < ε. This proves that χD : I → R is Riemann integrable. Since we have shown that there exists a partition P such that U(χD, P) < ε, we have vol (D) = Z I χD ≤ U(χD, P) < ε. Since ε > 0 is arbitrary, we find that vol(D) = 0. This completes the proof of (c) implies (a). Motivated by Theorem 6.30, we make the following definition. Definition 6.16 Jordan Content Zero Let D be a bounded subset of Rn. We say that D has Jordan content zero provided that for any ε > 0, there are finitely many closed rectangles I1, . . . ,Ik such that D ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij and kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 400 Sets that have Jordan Content Zero Let D be a bounded subset of Rn. Theorem 6.30 says that D is Jordan measurable with volume zero if and only if it has Jordan content zero. The characterization of sets with zero volume given in Theorem 6.30 facilitates the proofs of properties of such sets. Theorem 6.31 Let D1 and D2 be bounded subsets of Rn. If D1 has Jordan content zero and D2 ⊂ D1, then D2 also has Jordan content zero. Proof Given ε > 0, since D1 has Jordan content zero, there are closed rectangles I1, . . . ,Ik such that D1 ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij and kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε. Since D2 ⊂ D1, we find that D2 ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij and kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε. Therefore, D2 also has Jordan content zero. Example 6.25 Let D be the subset of R3 given by D = {(x, y, 2) | − 2 ≤ x ≤ 3, −5 ≤ y ≤ 7} . Show that D is a Jordan measurable set with zero volume.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 401 Solution Let I = [ −2, 3] × [−5, 7] × [2, 3]. Then I is a closed rectangle in R3. Example 6.24 says that ∂ I has Jordan content zero. Since D ⊂ ∂ I, Theorem 6.31 says that D has Jordan content zero. Hence, D is a Jordan measurable set with zero volume. The next theorem concerns unions and intersections of sets of Jordan content zero. Theorem 6.32 (a) If A = {Dα | α ∈ J} is a collection of sets that have Jordan content zero, then their intersection U = \ α∈J Dα also has Jordan content zero. (b) If D1, . . ., Dm are finitely many sets that have Jordan content zero, then their union D = m[ j=1 Dj is also a set that has Jordan content zero. Proof (a) is obvious since U ⊂ Dα for any α ∈ J. (b) is basically a consequence of the fact that finite union of finite sets is finite. Given ε > 0, for each 1 ≤ j ≤ m, since Dj has Jordan content zero, there is a finite collection Bj = {Iβj | βj ∈ Jj} of closed rectangles such that Dj ⊂ [ βj ∈Jj Iβj , X βj ∈Jj vol � Iβj  < ε m . Let B = m[ j=1 Bj. Since each Bj, 1 ≤ j ≤ m is finite, B is also a finite collection of closed rectangles. Moreover, D = m[ j=1 Dj ⊂ m[ j=1 [ βj ∈Jj Iβj = [ Iβ ∈B Iβ,Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 402 and X Iβ ∈B vol (Iβ) ≤ mX j=1 X βj ∈Jj vol � Iβj  < ε. This shows that D has Jordan content zero. Example 6.26 It is obvious that a one-point subset of Rn has Jordan content zero. It follows that any finite subset of Rn has Jordan content zero. Now we want to consider general Jordan measurable sets. We first prove the following two theorems, giving more examples of Riemann integrable functions. The first one is a special case of the second one, but we need to prove it first to prove the second theorem. Theorem 6.33 Let I = nY i=1 [ai, bi], and let f : I → R be a bounded function defined on I. If f : I → R is continuous on the interior of I, then f : I → R is Riemann integrable. Proof We will show that for any ε > 0, there is a partition P of I such that U(f, P) − L(f, P) < ε. Since f : I → R is a bounded function, there is a positive number M such that |f(x)| ≤ M for all x ∈ I. By Lemma 6.18, there is a closed rectangle ˇI = nY i=1 [ui, vi] contained in the interior of I, such that vol (I) − vol (ˇI) < ε 4M .Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 403 Let P0 = ( P1, . . . , Pn) be the partition of I given by Pi = {ai, ui, vi, bi} for 1 ≤ i ≤ n. Then ˇI is a rectangle in the partition P0. Since f : ˇI → R is continuous, Theorem 6.27 implies that there is a partition P1 of ˇI such that U(f, P1) − L(f, P1) < ε 2 . Let P be the partition of I so that it contains all the partition points in P0 and P1. Then P is a refinement of P0 and the partition that P induces on ˇI is P(ˇI) = P1. By Proposition 6.3, U(f, P) − L(f, P) = X J∈JP0 (U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J))) = U(f, P1) − L(f, P1) + X J∈JP0 \{ˇI} (U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J))) . For each J in JP0 \ {ˇI}, we use the crude estimate U(f, P(J) − L(f, P(J)) ≤ 2M vol (J). These imply that U(f, P) − L(f, P) < ε 2 + 2M X J∈JP0 \{ˇI} vol (J) = ε 2 + 2M � vol (I) − vol (ˇI)  < ε. Set of Discontinuities of a Function Given a function f : A → R defined on the set A, the set of discontinuities of f is the set of all points x0 in A such that f is not continuous at x0. If B is a subset of A, and x0 is a point of B, f : A → R is continuous at x0 implies that f : B → R is continuous at x0. Hence, the set of discontinuities of the function f : B → R is a subset of the set of discontinuities of the function f : A → R.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 404 Theorem 6.34 Let I = nY i=1 [ai, bi]. Given that f : I → R is a bounded function defined on I, let Nf be the set of discontinuities of f : I → R. If Nf is a set that has Jordan content zero, then f : I → R is Riemann integrable. Proof We will show that for any ε > 0, there is a partition P of I such that U(f, P) − L(f, P) < ε. Since f : I → R is a bounded function, there is a positive number M such that |f(x)| ≤ M for all x ∈ I. Since Nf is a set of Jordan content zero that is contained in I, there are closed rectangles I1, . . . ,Ik such that Nf ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij and kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε 4M . By Lemma 6.29, there is a partitionP0 of I such that each rectangleJ in the partition P0 is either contained in an Ij for some 1 ≤ j ≤ k, or is disjoint from the interiors of Ij for all 1 ≤ j ≤ k. Let A = {J ∈ JP0 | J ⊂ Ij for some 1 ≤ j ≤ k} , and B = {J ∈ JP0 | J ∩ int (Ij) = ∅ for all 1 ≤ j ≤ k} . Assume that B contains N rectangles. If J ∈ B, f : J → R is continuous on the interior of J. By Theorem 6.33, f : J → R is Riemann integrable. Therefore, there is a partition PJ of J such that U(f, PJ) − L(f, PJ) < ε 2N .Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 405 The rest of the proof is similar to the proof of Theorem 6.33. Let P be the partition of I which contains all the partition points in P0 and PJ for all J ∈ B. Then U(f, P) − L(f, P) = X J∈JP0 (U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J))) = X J∈A (U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J))) + X J∈B (U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J))) . For each J in A , we use the crude estimate U(f, P(J) − L(f, P(J)) ≤ 2M vol (J). Using the fact that [ J∈A J ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij, we have X J∈A (U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J))) ≤ 2M kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε 2 . For each J ∈ B, P(J) is a refinement of PJ, and thus U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J)) < ε 2N . This implies that X J∈B (U(f, P(J)) − L(f, P(J))) < ε 2 . These give us U(f, P) − L(f, P) < ε, as desired. Now we can prove the following characterization of Jordan measurable sets.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 406 Theorem 6.35 Let D be a bounded subset of Rn. The following are equivalent. (a) D is a Jordan measurable set. (b) The boundary of D has Jordan content zero. Proof Let I be a closed rectangle that contains D. By definition, D is Jordan measurable if and only if the function χD : I → R is Riemann integrable. By Theorem 6.28, the set of discontinuities of the function χD : I → R is the set ∂D. If the boundary of D has Jordan content zero, Theorem 6.34 implies that χD : I → R is Riemann integrable. This proves (b) implies (a). Conversely, if D is Jordan measurable, given ε > 0, there is a partition P such that U(χD, P) − L(χD, P) < ε 2 . For each J in JP, there are only three possibilities for the pair (mJ, MJ). Namely, (1, 1), (0, 0) or (0, 1). Let A = {J ∈ JP | mJ = MJ = 1} , B = {J ∈ JP | mJ = MJ = 0} , C = {J ∈ JP | mJ = 0, M J = 1} . Then JP = A ∪ B ∪ C , and we have U(χD, P) − L(χD, P) = X J∈JP (MJ − mJ) vol (J) = X J∈C vol (J). This implies that X J∈C vol (J) < ε 2 . Notice that J is in A if and only if f(x) = 1 for all x ∈ J, if and only if J ⊂ D. This implies that int J ⊂ int D for all J ∈ A .Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 407 Similarly, J is in B if and only if f(x) = 0 for all x ∈ J, if and only if J ⊂ Rn \ D. This implies that int J ⊂ ext D for all J ∈ B. Let S = [ J∈A ∪B ∂J. Since Rn is a disjoint union of int D, ext D and ∂ D, we must have ∂D ⊂  [ J∈C J ! ∪ S . Since the boundary of a closed rectangle has Jordan content zero, and A ∪ B is a finite set, Theorem 6.32 implies that S has Jordan content zero. Hence, there is a finite collection of rectangles D = {Ij | 1 ≤ j ≤ k} such that S ⊂ k[ j=1 Ij and kX j=1 vol (Ij) < ε 2 . Let E = C ∪ D. Then E is a finite collection of closed rectangles, ∂ D ⊂ [ J∈E J and X J∈E vol (J) < ε. This shows that D has Jordan content zero. Using Theorem 6.35, we can obtain more examples of Jordan measurable sets. First we prove the following. Lemma 6.36 Let A and B be subsets of Rn. Then ∂(A ∪ B) ⊂ ∂A ∪ ∂B, ∂ (A ∩ B) ⊂ ∂A ∪ ∂B.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 408 Proof If x0 be a point in ∂(A ∪ B), there is sequence of points {uk} in A ∪ B that converges to x0. Each point in this sequence is either in A or in B. Therefore, there is a subsequence {ukj } that is in A or in B. There is also a sequence {vk} in Rn \ (A ∪ B) that converges to x0. This sequence is in both Rn \ A and in Rn \ B. Therefore, x0 is in ∂A or in ∂B. If x0 is a point in∂(A∩B), there is sequence of points{uk} in Rn \(A∩B) that converges to x0. Each point in this sequence is either in Rn \ A or in Rn \ B. Therefore, there is a subsequence {ukj } that is in Rn \ A or in Rn \ B. There is also a sequence {vk} in A ∩ B that converges to x0. This sequence is in both A and B. Therefore, x0 is in ∂A or in ∂B. One is tempted to think that ∂(A ∩ B) ⊂ ∂A ∩ ∂B. But this is not true, as shown in the following example. Example 6.27 Let A = [0 , 2] × [0, 2] and B = [1 , 3] × [1, 3]. We find that A ∩ B = [1, 2] × [1, 2]. As shown in Figure 6.17, ∂A ∩ ∂B is a set with 4 points, ∂(A ∩ B) ̸= ∂A ∩ ∂B, but ∂(A ∩ B) ⊂ ∂A ∪ ∂B. Figure 6.17: ∂(A ∩ B) ̸= ∂A ∩ ∂B, but ∂(A ∩ B) ⊂ ∂A ∪ ∂B. Using Lemma 6.36, we obtain the following.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 409 Theorem 6.37 If D1, D2, . . ., Dm are Jordan measurable sets, then the set D1 ∩ D2 ∩ · · · ∩ Dm and the set D1 ∪ D2 ∪ · · · ∪ Dm are also Jordan measurable. Proof It suffices to prove the case where m = 2 . The general case follows by induction. If D1 and D2 are Jordan measurable, Theorem 6.35 says that ∂D1 and ∂D2 have Jordan content zero. Theorem 6.32 says that ∂D1 ∪ ∂D2 has Jordan content zero. Lemma 6.36 and Theorem 6.31 imply that ∂(D1 ∩ D2) and ∂(D1 ∪ D2) have Jordan content zero. Theorem 6.35 again implies that D1 ∩ D2 and D1 ∪ D2 are Jordan measurable sets. Observe that the concept of Jordan measurable sets and Riemann integrable functions are closely related. In a nutshell, a set D is a Jordan measurable set if and only if all the constant functions f : D → R are Riemann integrable. In fact, it is also if and only if all the continuous functions f : D → R are Riemann integrable. We can even allow discontinuities on a set that has Jordan content zero. We will prove this after a few preparatory remarks and lemmas. Remark 6.3 If D is a bounded subset of Rn and D is contained in the closed rectangle I, then D is also contained in I. This implies that ∂D is also contained in I. The following example depicts the relation between the set of dicontinuities of a function f : D → R and its zero extension. Example 6.28 Let D = {(x, y) | 0 ≤ y ≤ x ≤ 2}, and let f : D → R be the function defined as f(x, y) =    1, if (x, y) ∈ D and 0 ≤ x < 1, 2, if (x, y) ∈ D and 1 ≤ x ≤ 2.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 410 The set of discontinuities of the function f : D → R is Nf = {(1, y) | 0 ≤ y ≤ x}. The set D is contained in the rectangle I = [0, 2] × [0, 2]. Let ˇf : I → R be the zero extension of f : D → R. Then the set of discontinuities of ˇf : I → R is N ˇf = {(1, y) | 0 ≤ y ≤ 1} ∪ {(x, x) | 0 ≤ x ≤ 2}, which is a subset of Nf ∪ ∂D. Figure 6.18: The set of discontinuities of the functions discussed in Example 6.28. The following lemma gives the general case. Lemma 6.38 Let D be a bounded subset of Rn. Given that f : D → R is a bounded function defined on D, let ˇf : Rn → R be its zero extension. Let Nf = {x0 ∈ D | f : D → R is not continuous at x0} , N ˇf =   x0 ∈ Rn | ˇf : Rn → R is not continuous at x0 	 . Then N ˇf ⊂ ∂D ∪ N f .Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 411 Proof We will show that ˇf : Rn → R is continuous at x0 if x0 is not in ∂D ∪ N f. If x0 /∈ ∂D ∪ N f, there are two possibilities. • x0 is in ext D. • x0 is in int D and f : D → R is continuous at x0. If x0 is in ext D, there is an r > 0 such that B(x0, r) ⊂ Rn \ D. Since ˇf(x) = 0 for all x ∈ B(x0, r), ˇf : Rn → R is continuous at x0. If x0 is in int D and f : D → R is continuous at x0, we want to show that ˇf : Rn → R is continuous at x0. Given that {xk} is a sequence in Rn that converges to x0, there is a positive integer K such that xk ∈ int D for all k ≥ K. This implies that ˇf(xk) = f(xk) for all k ≥ K. Since f : D → R is continuous at x0, lim k→∞ f(xK+k) = f(x0). This implies that lim k→∞ ˇf(xk) = lim k→∞ ˇf(xK+k) = lim k→∞ f(xK+k) = f(x0) = ˇf(x0). Hence, ˇf : Rn → R is continuous at x0. This completes the proof. Now we can prove the main theorem. Theorem 6.39 Let D be a bounded subset of Rn. Given that f : D → R is a bounded function defined on D, let Nf be the set of discontinuities of f : D → R. If D is a Jordan measurable set, and Nf is a set that has Jordan content zero, then f : D → R is Riemann integrable. Proof Let I be a closed rectangle in Rn that contains D, and let ˇf : I → R be the zero extension of f : D → R to I. We want to show that ˇf : I → R is Riemann integrable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 412 By Lemma 6.38, the set of discontinuities of ˇf : I → R is contained in ∂D ∪ Nf. Since D is Jordan measurable, Theorem 6.35 says that ∂D has Jordan content zero. Theorem 6.32 and Theorem 6.31 then imply that the set N ˇf has Jordan content zero. By Theorem 6.34, ˇf : I → R is Riemann integrable. This completes the proof. In particular, we have the following. Corollary 6.40 Let D be a subset of Rn that is Jordan measurable, and let f : D → R be a bounded function defined on D. If f : D → R is continuous, then it is Riemann integrable. Let us emphasize the result in Corollary 6.40. Riemann Integrability of Continuous Functions Any continuous function defined on a Jordan measurable set is Riemann integrable. Another interesting corollary of Theorem 6.39 is the following. Corollary 6.41 Let D be a subset of Rn that has Jordan content zero. If f : D → R is a bounded function defined on D, then f : D → R is Riemann integrable and Z D f = 0 . Proof Since D has Jordan content zero, and the set Nf of discontinuities of f : D → R is a subset ofD, Nf has Jordan content zero. Theorem 6.39 implies that f : D → R is Riemann integrable. Since f : D → R is bounded, there is a positive number M such that −M ≤ f(x) ≤ M for all x ∈ D.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 413 By monotonicity theorem, Z D −M dx ≤ Z D f(x) dx ≤ Z D M dx. For any constant c, linearity implies that Z D c dx = c vol (D) = 0 . This proves that Z D f = 0 . Let us highlight this important result. Bounded Functions Defined on Sets that has Jordan Content Zero Any bounded function defined on a set that has Jordan content zero is Riemann integrable with integral zero. Corollary 6.41 also gives the following. Lemma 6.42 Let D be a bounded subset ofRn, and let f : D → R be a bounded function defined on D. If there is a subset A of D with Jordan content zero such that f(x) = 0 for all x ∈ D \ A, then f : D → R is Riemann integrable andZ D f = 0. Proof Let I be a closed rectangle in Rn that contains D, and let ˇf : I → R be the zero extension of the function f : D → R. Notice that it is also the zero extension of the function f |A : A → R. By Corollary 6.41, f |A : A → R is Riemann integrable and Z A f |A = 0 . Hence, ˇf : I → R is Riemann integrable, and so is f : D → R. Moreover, Z D f = Z I ˇf = Z A f |A = 0. Using Lemma 6.42, we obtain the following important result, which says thatChapter 6. Multiple Integrals 414 Riemann integrability is not affected by the definition of the function on a set that has Jordan content zero. Theorem 6.43 Let D be a bounded subset of Rn, and let f : D → R and g : D → R be bounded functions defined on D. If f : D → R is Riemann integrable and there is a subset A of D which has Jordan content zero such that g(x) = f(x) for all x ∈ D \ A, then g : D → R is Riemann integrable and Z D g = Z D f. Sketch of Proof Let h : D → R be the function h(x) = f(x) − g(x). Then h(x) = 0 for all x ∈ D \ A. By Lemma 6.42, h : D → R is Riemann integrable andZ D h = 0. The assertion follows from linearity. Using Theorem 6.43, we can generalize additivity to arbitrary sets. Theorem 6.44 Additivity Given that D1 and D2 are bounded subsets of Rn such that D1 ∩ D2 is a set that has Jordan content zero, let D = D1 ∪ D2. Assume that f : D → R is a bounded function defined on D. If the functions f : D1 → R and f : D2 → R are Riemann integrable, then the function f : D → R is Riemann integrable and Z D f = Z D1∪D2 f = Z D1 f + Z D2 f.Chapter 6. Theorem 6.45 Given that D1, D2, . . ., Dm are bounded subsets of Rn such that for any pairs of (i, j) with i ̸= j, Di ∩ Dj has Jordan content zero, let D = D1 ∪ D2 ∪ · · · ∪ Dm. Remark 6.4 If a function f : D → R is Riemann integrable and D1 is a subset of D, f : D1 → R is not necessarily Riemann integrable. For example, consider the constant function f on D = [0, 1]n which takes value 1. Its restriction to D1 = D ∩ Qn is not Riemann integrable. Corollary 6.46 Let D1, D2, . . ., Dm be Jordan measurable subsets of Rn such that for any pairs of (i, j) with i ̸= j, Di ∩ Dj has Jordan content zero. Then the set D = D1 ∪ D2 ∪ · · · ∪ Dm is also Jordan measurable. Moreover, vol (D) = vol (D1) + vol (D2) + · · · + vol (Dm). Let us return to explore more on Jordan measurable sets. So far we only know explicitly that a closed rectangle is Jordan measurable, and some examples of sets that have Jordan content zero. Since a bounded subset D is Jordan measurable if and only if its boundary has Jordan content zero, we will first explore sets that have Jordan content zero. The following theorem will give us a lots of examples of sets that have Jordan content zero. Theorem 6.47 Let D be a Jordan measurable subset of Rn, and let f : D → R be a Riemann integrable function. Then the graph of f defined by Gf =   (x, y) ∈ Rn+1 | x ∈ D, y = f(x) 	 is a subset of Rn+1 that has Jordan content zero. Proof Let I be a closed rectangle in Rn that contains D, and let ˇf : I → R be the zero extension of f : D → R. Fixed ε > 0. Since f : D → R is Riemann integrable, there is a partition P of I such that U( ˇf ,I) − L( ˇf ,I) < ε 2 . Let η = ε 4vol (I) .Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 417 Then η > 0. Let A = {J × [mJ − η, MJ + η] | J ∈ JP} . Then A is a finite collection of closed rectangles in Rn+1. If (x, f(x)) is in Gf, there is a J ∈ JP such that x ∈ J. Then mJ ≤ f(x) ≤ MJ implies that (x, f(x)) is in J × [mJ − η, MJ + η]. This proves that Gf ⊂ [ K∈A K. Now, X K∈A vol (K) = X J∈JP (MJ − mJ + 2η) vol (J) = U( ˇf ,P) − L( ˇf ,P) + 2η X J∈JP vol (J) < ε 2 + 2η vol (I) < ε. This proves that Gf has Jordan content zero. Specialize to continuous functions, we have the following. Corollary 6.48 Let D be a Jordan measurable set inRn, and let f : D → R be a continuous function. Then the graph of f defined by Gf =   (x, y) ∈ Rn+1 | x ∈ D, y = f(x) 	 is a subset of Rn+1 that has Jordan content zero. Example 6.29 Any line segment L between two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) in R2 has Jordan content zero. If x1 = x2, the line segment L is vertical. It is a subset of the boundary of the closed rectangle [x1, x1 + 1] × [y1, y2]. Hence, L has Jordan content zero.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 418 If x1 ̸= x2, L is the graph of the continuous function f : [x1, x2] → R, f(x) = y1 + y2 − y1 x2 − x1 (x − x1). Therefore L also has Jordan content zero. Figure 6.19: Line segments in the plane have Jordan content zero. Example 6.30 Since the boundary of a polygon in R2 is a finite union of line segments, a polygon is Jordan measurable. The interior of the polygon is also Jordan measurable as it has the same boundary. Figure 6.20: Polygons in the plane are Jordan measurable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 419 Example 6.31 We can argue that a line segment or the part of a plane in R3 contained inside a bounded set has Jordan content zero. A plane in R3 satisfies an equation of the form ax + by + cz = d, where (a, b, c) ̸= 0. Therefore, one can always solve one of the variables as a function of the other two. For example, if c ̸= 0, then z = f(x, y) = d − ax − by c . Hence, a plane is the graph of a continuous function. If we consider the part of the plane contained within a bounded set, then it must have Jordan content zero. For example, S = {(x, y, z) | x + y + z = 3, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0, z ≥ 0} is the part of the plane x + y + z = 3 bounded inside the rectangle [0, 3] × [0, 3] × [0, 3]. Hence, S has Jordan content zero. A line segment in R3 can always be regarded as a subset of a part of a plane that is contained in a bounded set. Hence, it also has Jordan content zero. Example 6.32 The boundary of the open rectangle U = nY i=1 (ai, bi) is the same as the boundary of its closure R = nY i=1 [ai, bi]. Hence, U is also a Jordan measurable set. Since U and ∂U are disjoint, and their union is R, vol U + vol ∂U = vol R. Since vol (∂U ) = 0, we have vol U = vol R. Motivated by Example 6.32, an interesting question to ask is if the subset DChapter 6. Multiple Integrals 420 of Rn is Jordan measurable, is its closure D Jordan measurable? This is answered in the following theorem. Theorem 6.49 If D is a subset of Rn that is Jordan measurable, so is D. Moreover, vol D = vol D. Proof First we claim that ∂D ⊂ ∂D. As the closure of D, D is a disjoint union of int D and ∂D. As the closure of D, D is a disjoint union of int D and ∂D. Since D ⊂ D, we have int D ⊂ int D. Hence, we must have ∂D ⊂ ∂D. If D is Jordan measurable, ∂D has Jordan content zero. Since ∂D ⊂ ∂D, ∂D also has Jordan content zero. Hence, D is Jordan measurable. For the last statement, we use the fact that D = D ∪ ∂D. Notice that D ∩ ∂D ⊂ ∂D. Hence, D ∩ ∂D has Jordan content zero. By the additivity theorem, vol D + vol ∂D = vol D. Since vol ∂D = 0, we conclude that vol D = vol D. Example 6.33 Consider the set A = (−1, 0) ∪ (0, 1). Its closure is A = [−1, 1]. Hence, ∂A = {−1, 1} is not equal to ∂A = {−1, 0, 1}. This also shows that even for an open set A, we does not have ∂A = ∂A. Remark 6.5 If D is a bounded subset of Rn such that D is Jordan measurable, one cannot deduce that D is Jordan measurable. An example is given by D = [0, 1]n ∩ Qn, which is not Jordan measurable, but D = [0 , 1]n is Jordan measurable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 421 Example 6.34 Let r be a positive number. Multiple Integrals 422 Example 6.35 Let [a, b] be a closed interval in R, and let f : [a, b] → R and g : [a, b] → R be continuous functions satisfying f(x) ≤ g(x) for all x ∈ [a, b]. Define D1 and D2 to be the sets D1 = {(x, y) | a < x < b, f (x) < y < g (x)} , D2 = {(x, y) | a ≤ x ≤ b, f(x) ≤ y ≤ g(x)} Show that D1 and D2 are Jordan measurable sets. Solution Since f : [ a, b] → R and g : [ a, b] → R are continuous, D1 is open and D2 is closed. Since [a, b] is compact, they are bounded. There is a positive number M such that |f(x)| ≤ M |g(x)| ≤ M for all x ∈ [a, b]. Let S1 = {(a, y) | − M ≤ y ≤ M } , S2 = {(b, y) | − M ≤ y ≤ M } , S3 = {(x, y) | a ≤ x ≤ b, y = f(x)} , S4 = {(x, y) | a ≤ x ≤ b, y = g(x)} , and let S = S1 ∪ S2 ∪ S3 ∪ S4. Then D2 \ D1 ⊂ S. Since D1 is open and D1 ⊂ D2, D1 = int D1 ⊂ int D2. Since D2 is closed and D1 ⊂ D2, D1 ⊂ D2 = D2.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 423 Therefore, ∂D1 = D1 \ int D1 ⊂ D2 \ D1 ⊂ S, ∂D2 = D2 \ int D2 ⊂ D2 \ D1 ⊂ S. Since S1 and S2 are line segments, they have Jordan content zero. Since S3 and S4 are graphs of continuous functions defined on the Jordan measurable set [a, b], S3 and S4 also have Jordan content zero. These imply that S has Jordan content zero. Thus, ∂D1 and ∂D2 also have Jordan content zero, which imply that D1 and D2 are Jordan measurable sets. Figure 6.22: The set D = {(x, y) | a ≤ x ≤ b, f(x) ≤ y ≤ g(x)} is Jordan measurable. More generally than Example 6.35, we can prove the following. Theorem 6.50 Let U be a Jordan measurable set in Rn, and let f : U → R and g : U → R be bounded continuous functions on U satisfying f(x) ≤ g(x) for all x ∈ U. Then the subsets D1 = {(x, y) | x ∈ U , f(x) < y < g (x)} , D2 = {(x, y) | x ∈ U , f(x) ≤ y ≤ g(x)} of Rn+1 are Jordan measurable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 424 Sketch of Proof Let M be a positive number such that |f(x)| ≤ M and |g(x)| ≤ M for all x ∈ U . The sets S1, S2 and S3 have Jordan content zero. Example 6.36 We claim that an open ball B(x0, r) in Rn and its closure are Jordan measurable sets. When n = 1, B1 = (−1, 1) is an interval whose boundary is the two point set {−1, 1} which has Jordan content zero. For n ≥ 1, assume that Bn is a Jordan measurable subset of Rn. Multiple Integrals 425 Example 6.37 Let D = {(x, y, z) | x2 + y2 < 4, −3 ≤ z ≤ 3}, and let f : D → R be the function defined as f(x, y, z) = x2 + 4y2 + 9z2. Multiple Integrals 426 Solution The set D is an open ball. Hence, it is Jordan measurable. Figure 6.24: The Jordan measurable set in Example 6.38. At the end of this section, let us prove a few interesting theorems. The next theorem shows that a set with Jordan content zero cannot have nonempty interior. Theorem 6.51 Let D be a subset of Rn that has Jordan content zero. Then int D = ∅. Proof We use proof by contradiction. If int D ̸= ∅, it is an open set that contains at least one point u = (u1, . . . , un). By definition of interior points, there exists r > 0 such that B(u, r) ⊂ D. There exists δ > 0 such that the rectangle Iδ = nY i=1 [ui − δ, ui + δ] is contained in B(u, r), and hence in D. Since D has Jordan content zero, we find that Iδ also has Jordan content zero. But vol (Iδ) = (2δ)n > 0. This gives a contradiction.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 427 The next one is the mean value theorem for integrals. Theorem 6.52 Mean Value Theorem for Integrals Let D be a closed and bounded Jordan measurable set in Rn, and let f : D → R be a continuous function. If D is connected or path-connected, then there is a point x0 in D such that Z D f(x)dx = f(x0) vol (D). Proof Since D is compact and f : D → R is continuous, extreme value theorem asserts that there exist points u and v in D such that f(u) ≤ f(x) ≤ f(v) for all x ∈ D. Since D is a Jordan measurable set and f : D → R is continuous, f : D → R is Riemann integrable. The monotonicity theorem implies that f(u) vol (D) ≤ Z D f(x)dx ≤ f(v) vol (D). If vol (D) = 0, we can take x0 to be any point in D. If vol (D) ̸= 0, notice that c = 1 vol (D) Z D f(x)dx satisfies f(u) ≤ c ≤ f(v). Since D is connected or path-connected, and f : D → R is continuous, intermediate value theorem asserts that f(D) must be an interval. Since f(u) and f(v) are in f(D) and c is in between them, c must also be in f(D). This means that there is an x0 in D such that 1 vol (D) Z D f(x)dx = c = f(x0).Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 431 6.4 Iterated Integrals and Fubini’s Theorem In Section 6.3, we have given a sufficient condition for a function f : D → R to be Riemann integrable. Riemann Integrable Functions If D is a subset of Rn such that a constant function on D is Riemann integrable, then any bounded function on D whose set of discontinuities is a set that has Jordan content zero is Riemann integrable. However, we have not discussed any strategy to compute a Riemann integral, except by using a sequence of partitions {Pk} with lim k→∞ |Pk| = 0. This is a practical approach if one has a computer, but it is not feasible for hand calculations. Besides, it might also be difficult for us to understand the dependence of the integral on the parameters in the integrand. When n = 1 , we have seen that the fundamental theorem of calculus gives us a powerful tool to calculate a Riemann integral when the integrand is a continuous function that has explicit antiderivatives. To be able to apply this powerful tool in the multivariable context, we need to relate multiple integrals with iterated integrals. This is the topic that is studied in this section. As a motivation, consider a continuous functionf : [a, b] × [c, d] → R defined on the closed rectangle I = [a, b] × [c, d] in R2. If P = (P1, P2) is a partition of I with P1 = {x0, x1, . . . , xk} and P2 = {y0, y1, . . . , yl}, there are kl rectangles in the partition P. Denote the rectangles by Ji,j with 1 ≤ i ≤ k and 1 ≤ j ≤ l, where Ji,j = [xi−1, xi] × [yj−1, yj]. Choose a set of intermediate points A = {αi} for the partition P1, and a set of intermediate points B = {βj} for the partition P2. Let ξi,j = (αi, βj) for 1 ≤ i ≤ k, 1 ≤ j ≤ l.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 432 Then C =   ξi,j | 1 ≤ i ≤ k, 1 ≤ j ≤ l 	 is a choice of intermediate points for the partition P. The Riemann sum R(f, P, C) is given by R(f, P, C) = kX i=1 lX j=1 f(αi, βj)(xi − xi−1)(yj − yj−1). (6.4) Since it is a finite sum, it does not matter which order we perform the summation. For fixed x ∈ [a, b], let gx : [c, d] → R be the function gx(y) = f(x, y), y ∈ [c, d]. If we perform the sum over j in (6.4) first, we find that R(f, P, C) = kX i=1  lX j=1 gαi(βj)(yj − yj−1) ! (xi − xi−1) = kX i=1 R(gαi, P2, B)(xi − xi−1). Since gαi : [c, d] → R is continuous, it is Riemann integrable. Therefore, lim |P2|→0 R(gαi, P2, B) = Z d c gαi(y)dy. This prompts us to define the function F : [a, b] → R by F (x) = Z d c gx(y)dy = Z d c f(x, y)dy. Then lim |P2|→0 R(f, P, C) = kX i=1 lim |P2|→0 R(gαi, P2, B)(xi − xi−1) = kX i=1 F (αi)(xi − xi−1) = R(F, P1, A). If F : [a, b] → R is also Riemann integrable, we would have lim |P1|→0 lim |P2|→0 R(f, P, C) = lim |P1|→0 R(F, P1, A) = Z b a F (x)dx = Z b a Z d c f(x, y)dy  dx.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 433 Interchanging the roles of ofx and y, or equivalently, summing overi first in (6.4), we find that lim |P2|→0 lim |P1|→0 R(f, P, C) = Z d c Z b a f(x, y)dx  dy. Figure 6.25: Given a partition P of a rectangle, one can sum over the rectangles row by row, or column by column. Since |P| = p |P1|2 + |P2|2, |P| → 0 if and only if (|P1|, |P2|) → (0, 0). The question becomes whether the two limits lim |P1|→0 lim |P2|→0 R(f, P, C) and lim |P2|→0 lim |P1|→0 R(f, P, C) are equal; and whether they are equal to the limit lim (|P1|,|P2|)→(0,0) R(f, P, C). Remark 6.6 Let f : R2 → R be the function defined as f(x, y) = x2 x2 + y2 , (x, y) ∈ R2 \ {(0, 0)}.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 434 We find that lim y→0 lim x→0 f(x, y) = lim y→0 0 = 0, lim x→0 lim y→0 f(x, y) = lim x→0 1 = 1. Hence, lim y→0 lim x→0 f(x, y) ̸= lim x→0 lim y→0 f(x, y). This example shows that we cannot simply interchange the order of limits. In fact, the limit lim (x,y)→(0,0) f(x, y) does not exist. The integrals Z d c Z b a f(x, y)dx  dy and Z b a Z d c f(x, y)dy  dx are called iterated integrals. Definition 6.17 Iterated Integrals Let n be a positive integer larger than 1, and let k be a positive integer less than n. Denote a point in Rn by (x, y), where x ∈ Rk and y ∈ Rn−k. Given that I = nY i=1 [ai, bi] is a closed rectangle in Rn, let Ix = kY i=1 [ai, bi] and Iy = nY i=k+1 [ai, bi]. If f : I → R is a bounded function defined on I, an interated integral is an integral of the form Z Iy Z Ix f(x, y)dxdy or Z Ix Z Iy f(x, y)dydx, whenever they exist. Let us consider the following example.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 435 Example 6.39 Let g : [a, b] → R and h : [a, b] → R be continuous functions defined on [a, b] such that g(x) ≤ h(x) for all x ∈ [a, b]. Consider the set D defined as D = {(x, y) | a ≤ x ≤ b, g(x) ≤ y ≤ h(x)} . Let χD : R2 → R be the corresponding characteristic function. If c ≤ g(x) ≤ h(x) ≤ d for all x ∈ [a, b], then I = [a, b] × [c, d] is a closed rectangle that contains D. We have seen that D is a Jordan measurable set. Hence, χD : I → R is a Riemann integrable function. For any x ∈ [a, b], Z d c χD(x, y)dy = Z h(x) g(x) dy = h(x) − g(x). Therefore, the iterated integral Z b a Z d c χD(x, y)dy  dx is equal to Z b a Z d c χD(x, y)dy  dx = Z b a (h(x) − g(x)) dx. In single variable calculus, we have learned that the integralZ b a (h(x) − g(x)) dx gives the area of D. Thus, in this case, we have Z b a Z d c χD(x, y)dy  dx = vol (D) = Z [a,b]×[c,d] χD(x, y)dxdy. Namely, the iterated integral is equal to the double integral. The following theorem is the general case when n = 2.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 436 Figure 6.26: The domain D = {(x, y) | a ≤ x ≤ b, g(x) ≤ y ≤ h(x)}. Theorem 6.53 Fubini’s Theorem in the Plane Let I = [a, b] × [c, d], and let f : I → R be a Riemann integrable function. For each x ∈ [a, b], define the function gx : [c, d] → R by gx(y) = f(x, y), y ∈ [c, d]. If gx : [c, d] → R is Riemann integrable for each x ∈ [a, b], let F : [a, b] → R be the function defined as F (x) = Z d c gx(y)dy = Z d c f(x, y)dy. Then we have the following. (a) The function F : [a, b] → R is Riemann integrable. (b) The integral of f : [ a, b] × [c, d] → R is equal to the integral of F : [a, b] → R. Namely, Z [a,b]×[c,d] f = Z b a F. Equivalently, Z [a,b]×[c,d] f(x, y)dxdy = Z b a Z d c f(x, y)dydx.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 437 Proof Let I = Z [a,b]×[c,d] f(x, y)dxdy. We will show that for any ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that if P is a partition of [a, b] with |P | < δ , and A = {αi} is any set of intermediate points for P , then |R(F, P, A) − I| < ε. This will prove both (a) and (b). Fixed ε > 0. Since f : [ a, b] × [c, d] → R is Riemann integrable with integral I, there exists δ0 > 0 such that if P = ( P1, P2) is a partition of [a, b] × [c, d] with |P| < δ0, then U(f, P) − L(f, P) < ε. Take δ = δ0/2. Let P = {x0, x1, . . . , xk} be a partition of [a, b] with |P | < δ. Take any partition P2 = {y0, y1, . . . , yl} of [c, d] such that |P2| < δ . Let P = (P1, P2), where P1 = P . Then |P| < √ 2δ < δ 0. For 1 ≤ i ≤ k, 1 ≤ j ≤ l, let mi,j = inf (x,y)∈[xi−1,xi]×[yj−1,yj] f(x, y), Mi,j = sup (x,y)∈[xi−1,xi]×[yj−1,yj] f(x, y). Then L(f, P) = kX i=1 lX j=1 mi,j(xi − xi−1)(yj − yj−1), U(f, P) = kX i=1 lX j=1 Mi,j(xi − xi−1)(yj − yj−1). Now let A = {αi} be any choice of intermediate points for the partition P = P1. Notice that for any 1 ≤ i ≤ k, additivity theorem says that F (αi) = lX j=1 Z yj yj−1 gαi(y)dy = lX j=1 Z yj yj−1 f(αi, y)dy.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 438 Since mi,j ≤ f(αi, y) ≤ Mi,j for all y ∈ [yj−1, yj], we find that for 1 ≤ j ≤ l, mi,j(yi − yj−1) ≤ Z yj yj−1 f(αi, y)dy ≤ Mi,j(yi − yj−1). It follows that lX j=1 mi,j(yj − yj−1) ≤ F (αi) ≤ lX j=1 Mi,j(yj − yj−1). Multiply by (xi − xi−1), and sum over i from 1 to k, we find that L(f, P) ≤ kX i=1 F (αi)(xi − xi−1) ≤ U(f, P). In other words, L(f, P) ≤ R(F, P, A) ≤ U(f, P). Since we also have L(f, P) ≤ I ≤ U(f, P), we find that |R(F, P, A) − I| ≤ U(f, P) − L(f, P) < ε. This completes the proof. Example 6.40 Evaluate the integral Z [0,1]×[0,1] x sin(xy)dxdy. Solution The function f : [0 , 1] × [0, 1] → R, f(x, y) = x sin(xy) is a continuous function. Hence, it is Riemann integrable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 439 For each x ∈ [0, 1], the function gx : [0, 1] → R, gx(y) = x sin(xy) is also continuous. Hence, gx : [0 , 1] → R is Riemann integrable. By Fubini’s theorem, Z [0,1]×[0,1] x sin(xy)dxdy = Z 1 0 Z 1 0 x sin(xy)dydx = Z 1 0 [− cos(xy)]y=1 y=0 dx = Z 1 0 (1 − cos x) dx = 1 − [sin x]1 0 = 1 − sin 1. The roles of x and y in Fubini’s theorem can be interchanged, and we obain the following. Corollary 6.54 Assume that f : [a, b] × [c, d] → R is a Riemann integrable function such that for each x ∈ [a, b], the function gx : [ c, d] → R, gx(y) = f(x, y) is Riemann integrable; and for each y ∈ [c, d], the function hy : [a, b] → R, hy(x) = f(x, y) is Riemann integrable. Then we can interchange the order of integration. Namely, Z d c Z b a f(x, y)dxdy = Z b a Z d c f(x, y)dydx. Example 6.41 If we evaluate the iterated integral Z 1 0 Z 1 0 x sin(xy)dxdy directly, it would be quite tedious as we need to apply integration by parts to evaluate the integral Z 1 0 x sin(xy)dx. Using Corollary 6.54, we can interchange the order of integration and obtain Z 1 0 Z 1 0 x sin(xy)dxdy = Z 1 0 Z 1 0 x sin(xy)dydx = 1 − sin 1.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 440 Remark 6.7 The assumption thatf : [a, b]×[c, d] → R is Riemann integrable is essential in Fubini’s theorem. It does not follow from the fact that for eachx ∈ [a, b], the function gx : [ c, d] → R is Riemann integrable, and the function F : [a, b] → R, F (x) = Z d c gx(y)dy is Riemann integrable. For example, let g : [−1, 1] → R and h : [−1, 1] → R be the functions defined as g(x) =    1, if x is rational, −1, if x is irrational, h(y) =    1, if y ≥ 0, −1, if y < 0. . Then define the function f : [−1, 1] × [−1, 1] → R by f(x, y) = g(x)h(y). Since h : [−1, 1] → R is a step function, it is Riemann integrable and Z 1 −1 h(y)dy = Z 0 −1 h(y)dy + Z 1 0 h(y)dy = 0. Hence, for fixed x ∈ [−1, 1], Z 1 −1 f(x, y)dy = 0. Thus, the function F : [−1, 1] → R, F (x) = Z 1 −1 f(x, y)dy, being a function that is always zero, is Riemann integrable with integral 0. It follows that Z 1 −1 Z 1 −1 f(x, y)dydx = 0. However, one can prove that the function f : [ −1, 1] × [−1, 1] → R is not Riemann integrable, using the same way that we show that a Dirichlet’s function is not Riemann integrable.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 441 The fact that for each x ∈ [a, b], the function gx : [ c, d] → R, gx(y) = f(x, y) is Riemann integrable also does not follow from the fact that f : [a, b]×[c, d] → R is Riemann integrable. Consider for example the function f : [−1, 1] × [0, 1] → R, f(x, y) =    1, if x = 0 and y is rational, 0, otherwise. Then the set of discontinuities N of f : [ −1, 1] × [0, 1] → R is the line segment between the point (0, 0) and the point (0, 1). Hence, N has Jordan content 0. Therefore, f : [−1, 1] × [0, 1] → R is Riemann integrable. For x = 0, g0 : [0, 1] → R is the Dirichlet’s function. Hence, g0 : [0, 1] → R is not Riemann integrable. Now we consider the case depicted in Example 6.39 for more general functions. Theorem 6.55 Let g : [a, b] → R and h : [a, b] → R be continuous functions defined on [a, b] such that g(x) ≤ h(x) for all x ∈ [a, b], and let D be the set D = {(x, y) | a ≤ x ≤ b, g(x) ≤ y ≤ h(x)} . If f : D → R is a continuous function, then Z D f(x, y)dxdy = Z b a Z h(x) g(x) f(x, y)dydx. Proof Since g and h are continuous functions, there exist numbers c and d such that c ≤ g(x) ≤ h(x) ≤ d for all x ∈ [a, b]. Then I = [ a, b] × [c, d] be a closed rectangle that contains D. Hence, gx : [c, d] → R is Riemann integrable and for x ∈ [a, b], F (x) = Z d c gx(y)dy = Z h(x) g(x) f(x, y)dy. Again, the roles of x and y in Theorem 6.55 can be interchanged. Let us look at the following example. Now let us generalize the Fubini’s theorem to arbitrary positive integern that is larger than 1.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 445 Theorem 6.56 Fubini’s Theorem Let n be a positive integer larger than 1, and let k be a positive integer less than n. Given that I = nY i=1 [ai, bi] is a closed rectangle in Rn, let Ix = kY i=1 [ai, bi] and Iy = nY i=k+1 [ai, bi]. Assume that f : I → R is a Riemann integrable function such that for each x in Ix, the function gx : Iy → R defined by gx(y) = f(x, y), y ∈ Iy is Riemann integrable. Let F : Ix → R be the function defined as F (x) = Z Iy gx(y)dy = Z Iy f(x, y)dy. Then we have the followings. (a) The function F : Ix → R is Riemann integrable. (b) The integral of f : I → R is equal to the integral of F : Ix → R. Namely, Z I f(x, y)dxdy = Z Ix Z Iy f(x, y)dydx. (c) For each y in Iy, define the function hy : Ix → R by hy(x) = f(x, y), x ∈ Ix. If the function hy : Ix → R is Riemann integrable for each y ∈ Iy, then we can interchange the order of integration. Namely, Z Ix Z Iy f(x, y)dydx = Z Iy Z Ix f(x, y)dxdy. The proof is similar to the n = 2 case and we leave it to the readers. A useful case is the following which generalizes Theorem 6.55.Chapter 6. Consider the subset D of Rn defined as D = {(x, y) | x ∈ U , g(x) ≤ y ≤ h(x)} . If f : D → R is a bounded continuous function, then it is Riemann integrable, and Z D f(x, y)dxdy = Z U Z h(x) g(x) f(x, y)dydx, Let us look at an example. Multiple Integrals 450 6.5 Change of Variables Theorem Consider the problem of evaluating an integral of the form Z D f(x, y)dxdy when D is the disc D = {(x, y) | x2 + y2 ≤ r2}. When f : D → R is a continuous function, Fubini’s theorem says that we can write the integral as Z D f(x, y)dxdy = Z r −r Z √ r2−x2 − √ r2−x2 f(x, y)dydx. However, it is usually quite complicated to evaluate this integral due to the square roots. In some sense, we have not fully utilized the circular symmetry of the region of integration D. For regions that have circular symmetry, it might be easier if we use polar coordinates (r, θ) instead of rectangular coordinates (x, y). The goal of this section is to discuss the change of variables formula for multiple integrals. For single variable functions, the change of variable formula is usually known as integration by substitution. We have proved the following theorem in volume I. Theorem 6.58 Integration by Substitution Let ψ : [a, b] → R be a function that satisfies the following conditions: (i) ψ is continuous and one-to-one on [a, b]; (ii) ψ is continuously differentiable on (a, b); (iii) ψ′(x) is bounded on (a, b). If ψ([a, b]) = [ c, d], and f : [ c, d] → R is a bounded function that is continuous on (c, d), then the function h : [a, b] → R, h(x) = f(ψ(x))|ψ′(x)| is Riemann integrable and Z d c f(u)du = Z b a f(ψ(x))|ψ′(x)|dx. The function ψ : [ a, b] → R that satisfies all the three conditions (i)–(iii) in Theorem 6.58 defines a smooth change of variables u = ψ(x) from x to u.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 451 Definition 6.18 Smooth Change of Variables Let O be an open subset of Rn. A mapping Ψ : O → Rn from O to Rn is called a smooth change of variables provided that it satisfies the following conditions. (i) Ψ : O → Rn is one-to-one. (ii) Ψ : O → Rn is continuously differentiable. (iii) For each x ∈ O, the derivative matrix DΨ(x) is invertible. Remark 6.8 If the mapping Ψ : O → Rn is continuously differentiable, and the derivative matrix DΨ(x) is invertible for each x ∈ O, the inverse function theorem implies that the mapping Ψ : O → Rn is locally one-to-one. However, it might not be globally one-to-one. For Ψ : O → Rn to be a smooth change of variables, we need to impose the additional condition that it is globally one-to-one. Example 6.45 Let x0 be a point in Rn. The mapping Ψ : Rn → Rn, Ψ(x) = x + x0 is a smooth change of variables. It is one-to-one, continuously differentiable, and the derivative matrix is DΨ(x) = In, which is invertible. Example 6.46 Let x0 and y0 be points in Rn, and let A be an invertible n × n matrix. The mapping Ψ : Rn → Rn defined by Ψ(x) = y0 + A(x − x0) is a one-to-one continuously differentiable mapping. Its derivative matrix is DΨ(x) = A, which is invertible for all x in Rn. This shows that Ψ is a smooth change of variables. The mapping Ψ : Rn → Rn in Example 6.46 is a composition of translationsChapter 6. Multiple Integrals 452 and an invertible linear transformation. Example 6.47 Let O = {(x, y) | x > 0, y > 0}, and let Ψ : O → R2 be the mapping defined as Ψ(x, y) = (x2 − y2, 2xy). Show that Ψ : O → R2 is a smooth change of variables. Solution First we show that Ψ is one-to-one. If Ψ(x1, y1) = Ψ(x2, y2), then x2 1 − y2 1 = x2 2 − y2 2, 2x1y1 = 2x2y2. Let z1 = x1 + iy1 and z2 = x2 + iy2. Then we find that z2 1 = x2 1 − y2 1 + 2ix1y1 = x2 2 − y2 2 + 2ix2y2 = z2 2. Hence, we must have z2 = ±z1. Restricted to O, x1, x2, y1, y2 > 0. Hence, we must have z1 = z2, or equivalently, (x1, y1) = (x2, y2). This shows that Ψ : O → R2 is one-to-one. Now DΨ(x, y) = " 2x −2y 2y 2x # is continuous, and det DΨ(x, y) = 4 x2 + 4y2 ̸= 0 for all (x, y) ∈ O . This proves that Ψ is continuously differentiable and the derivative matrix DΨ(x, y) is invertible for all (x, y) ∈ O. Hence, Ψ : O → R2 is a smooth change of variables. In this section, we will state the change of variables theorem, and give some discusssions about why this theorem holds. We will also look at examples of how this theorem is applied, especially for polar and spherical coordinates. The proof of the theorem is quite technical and will be given in next section.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 453 Theorem 6.59 The Change of Variables Theorem Let O be an open subset of Rn, and let Ψ : O → Rn be a smooth change of variables. If D is a Jordan measurable set such that its closure D is contained in O, then Ψ(D) is also Jordan measurable. If f : Ψ(D) → R is a bounded continuous function, then the function g : D → R defined as g(x) = f (Ψ(x)) |det DΨ(x)| is Riemann integrable, and Z Ψ(D) f(x)dx = Z D g(x)dx = Z D f (Ψ(x)) |det DΨ(x)| dx. (6.5) Notice that the two vertical lines on det DΨ(x) in (6.5) means the absolute value, not the determinant. Remark 6.9 Jacobian For a mapping Ψ : O → Rn from a subset of Rn to Rn, the derivative matrix DΨ(x) is also called the Jacobian matrix of the mapping Ψ. The determinant of the Jacobian matrix is denoted by ∂(Ψ1, . . . ,Ψn) ∂(x1, . . . , xn) . It is known as theJacobian determinant, or simply asJacobian. In practice, we will often denote a change of variables Ψ : O → Rn by u = Ψ(x). Then the Jacobian can be written as ∂(u1, . . . , un) ∂(x1, . . . , xn) . Multiple Integrals 454 6.5.1 Translations and Linear Transformations In the single variable case, a translation is a map T : R → R, T (x) = x + c. If f : [a, b] → R is a Riemann integrable function, then Z b a f(x)dx = Z b−c a−c f(x + c)dx. For n ≥ 2, we have the following theorem, which is a stronger version of Theorem 6.59. Theorem 6.60 Let x0 be a fixed point in Rn, and let Ψ : Rn → Rn be the translation Ψ(x) = x + x0. If D is a Jordan measurable subset of Rn, then Ψ(D) is Jordan measurable. If f : Ψ(D) → R is a Riemann integrable function, then g = (f ◦ Ψ) : D → R is also Riemann integrable, and Z D+x0 f(x)dx = Z Ψ(D) f(x)dx = Z D g(x)dx = Z D f(x + x0)dx. (6.6) Proof Obviously, translation maps a rectangle to a rectangle with the same volume. Hence, it maps sets that have Jordan content zero to sets that have Jordan content zero. It is also obvious that Ψ maps the boundary of D to the boundary of Ψ(D). This shows that Ψ(D) is Jordan measurable. If I is a closed rectangle that contains D, then I′ = I + x0 is a closed rectangle that contains Ψ(D). Let ˇf : I′ → R and ˇg : I → R be the zero extensions of f : Ψ(D) → R and g = (f ◦Ψ) : D → R respectively. Then ˇg = ˇf ◦ Ψ. Since f : Ψ(D) → R is a Riemann integrable, Z I′ ˇf = Z I′ ˇf = Z I′ ˇf = Z Ψ(D) f. Given a partition P′ = ( P ′ 1, . . . , P′ n) of I′, let P = ( P1, . . . , Pn) be the partition of I induced by the translation Ψ. Namely, P is a partition such that the rectangle J is in JP if and only if J′ = Ψ(J) = J + x0 is in P′.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 455 Then mJ(ˇg) = inf {g(x) | x ∈ J} = inf {f(x + x0) | x ∈ J} = inf {f(x) | x′ ∈ J + x0} = mJ′( ˇf). It follows that L(ˇg, P) = X J∈JP mJ(ˇg) vol (J) = X J′∈JP′ mJ′( ˇf) vol (J′) = L( ˇf ,P′). Similarly, we have U(ˇg, P) = U( ˇf ,P′). Thus, the sets SL(ˇg) and SL( ˇf) of lower sums of ˇg and ˇf are the same, and the sets SU(ˇg) and SU( ˇf) of upper sums ofˇg and ˇf are also the same. These imply that Z I ˇg = sup SL(ˇg) = sup SL( ˇf) = Z I′ ˇf = Z Ψ(D) f, Z I ˇg = inf SU(ˇg) = inf SU( ˇf) = Z I′ ˇf = Z Ψ(D) f. Hence, g : D → I is Riemann integrable and Z D g = Z I ˇg = Z Ψ(D) f. Therefore, Z D (4x + y)dxdy = 5 Z u2+v2≤16 dudv. In single variable analysis, we have shown that the area of a disc of radius r is πr2. Hence, Z D (4x + y)dxdy = 5 × area (B) = 5 × 16π = 80π. Now we consider a linear transformation T : Rn → Rn, T(x) = Ax defined by an invertible matrix A. Since DT(x) = A, the change of variables theorem says that for any function f : D → R that is bounded and continuous on D, Z T(D) f(x)dx = Z D f(T(x))| det A|dx = | det A| Z D f(T(x))dx. (6.7) In the special case where f is a constant function, we have vol (T(D)) = | det A| vol (D). A very crucial fact to the proof of the change of variables theorem is a special case of this formula when D is a rectangle. Theorem 6.61 Let I = nY i=1 [ai, bi], and let T : Rn → Rn, T(x) = Ax be an invertible linear transformation. Then vol (T(I)) = | det A| vol (I). (6.8) Linear transformations map linear objects to linear objects. However, the image of a rectangle under a linear transformation is not necessary a rectangle, but is always a parallelepiped. If I is the closed rectangle nY i=1 [ai, bi], a point x in IChapter 6. Multiple Integrals 458 can be written as x = a + t1(b1 − a1)e1 + · · · + tn(bn − an)en, where a = ( a1, . . . , an), and t = ( t1, . . . , tn) ∈ [0, 1]n. Hence, we say that the rectangle I is a parallelepiped based at the point a and spanned by the n-linearly independent vectors vi = (bi − ai)ei, 1 ≤ i ≤ n. Definition 6.19 Parallelepipeds A (closed) parallelepiped in Rn is a solid P in Rn based at a point a and spanned by n-linearly independent vectors v1, . . . ,vn. It can be described as P = {a + t1v1 + · · · + tnvn | t = (t1, . . . , tn) ∈ [0, 1]n} . Figure 6.31: Parallelepipeds in R2 and R3. The boundary of a parallelepiped is a union of 2n bounded subsets, each of them is contained in a hyperplane. Thus, the boundary of a parallelepiped has Jordan content zero. Therefore, a parallelepiped is a Jordan measurable set. If P be a parallelepiped in Rn based at the pointa and spanned by the vectors v1, . . . ,vn, and T : Rn → Rn is an invertible linear transformation, then T(P) is the parallelepiped in Rn based at the point T(a) and spanned by the vectors T(v1), . . . ,T(vn). The cube [0, 1]n is called the standard unit cube and it is often denoted by Qn. If P is a parallelepiped in Rn based at the point a and spanned by the vectors v1, . . . ,vn, then P = Ψ(Qn), where Ψ : Rn → Rn is the mapping Ψ(x) = Ax + a, A = h v1 · · · vn i ,Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 459 which is a composition of an invertible linear transformation and a translation. Theorem 6.61 says that vol (P) = | det A|, (6.9) where A is the matrix whose column vectors are v1, . . . ,vn. For example, for a parallelogram in R2 which is spanned by the vectors v1 = " a1 b1 # and v2 = " a2 b2 # , the area of the parallogram is det " a1 a2 b1 b2 # . For a parallelepiped in R3 which is spanned by the vectors v1 =   a1 b1 c1   , v2 =   a2 b2 c2   and v3 =   a3 b3 c3   , the volume of the parallelepiped is det   a1 a1 a3 b1 b2 b3 c1 c2 c3   . These formulas have been derived in an elementary course. For general n, we will prove (6.9) in Appendix B using geometric arguments. This will then imply Theorem 6.61. From the theory of linear algebra, we know that an invertible matrix is a product of elementary matrices. Hence, an invertible linear transformation T : Rn → Rn can be written as T = Tm ◦ · · · ◦ T2 ◦ T1, where T1, T2, . . . ,Tm is one of the three types of elementary transformations, corresponding to the three types of elementary matrices.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 460 I. When E is the elementary matrix obtained from the identity matrix In by interchanging two distinct rows i and j, the linear tranformation T : Rn → Rn, T(x) = Ex interchanges xi and xj, and fixes the other variables. In this case, det E = −1 and | det E| = 1. II. When E is the elementary matrix obtained from the identity matrix In by multiplying row i by a nonzero constant c, the linear transformation T : Rn → Rn, T(x) = Ex maps the point x = (x1, . . . , xn) to T(x) = (x1, . . . , xi−1, cxi, xi+1, . . . , xn). In this case, det E = c, and | det E| = |c|. III. When E is the elementary matrix obtained from the identity matrix In by adding a constant c times of row j to another row i, the linear transformation T : Rn → Rn, T(x) = Ex maps the point x = (x1, . . . , xn) to T(x) = (x1, . . . , xi−1, xi + cxj, xi+1, . . . , xn). In this case, det E = 1, and | det E| = 1. Since each of the elementary transformations involves changes in at most two variables, it is sufficient to consider these transformations when n = 2. In any case, we find that vol (I′) = |k| vol (I).Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 462 Example 6.51 Let T : R2 → R2 be the linear transformation T(x, y) = (x + ky, y). Under this transformation, the rectangle I = [ a, b] × [c, d] is mapped to the parallepiped P with vertices (a + kc, c), (a + kd, d), (b + kc, c) and (b + kd, d). Using elementary geometric argument, one can show that vol (P) = vol (I). Combining Example 6.49, Example 6.50 and Example 6.51, we conclude that (6.8) holds when T : Rn → Rn is an elementary transformation. The type II elementary transformations maps rectangles to rectangles, so do their compositions. Therefore, (6.8) also holds if the linear transformation T : Rn → Rn is a composition of type II elementary transformations. This gives the following. Notice that r = p x2 + y2. Restricted to V = {(r, θ) | r > 0, 0 ≤ θ < 2π} , the map Φ : V → R2, Φ(r, θ) = (r cos θ, r sin θ) is one-to-one, and its range is R2 \ {(0, 0)}. However, the inverse of Φ fails to be continuous. We can extend the map Φ to R2 continuously. Namely, given (r, θ) ∈ R2, let (x, y) = Φ(r, θ), where x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ. Then Φ : R2 → R2 is continuously differentiable, but it fails to be one-to-one. Nevertheless, for any real number α, the map is continuous and one-to-one on the open set Oα = {(r, θ) | r > 0, α < θ < α + 2π} .Chapter 6. The change of variables theorem says the following.Chapter 6. Multiple Integrals 469 Theorem 6.63 Let [α, β] be a closed interval such that β ≤ α + 2π. Assume that g : [α, β] → R and h : [α, β] → R are continuous functions satisfying 0 ≤ g(θ) ≤ h(θ) for all α ≤ θ ≤ β. Let D be the region in the (x, y)-plane given by D = {(r cos θ, r sin θ) | α ≤ θ ≤ β, g(θ) ≤ r ≤ h(θ)} . If f : D → R is a continuous function, then Z D f(x, y)dxdy = Z β α Z h(θ) g(θ) f(r cos θ, r sin θ)rdrdθ. (6.11) Proof Let U = {(r, θ) | α ≤ θ ≤ β, g(θ) ≤ r ≤ h(θ)} . Then U is a compact Jordan measurable set in R2. If β < α + 2π, take any α0 such that α0 < α < β < α 0 + 2π. For example, we can take α0 = α − 2π − (β − α) 2 . If we also have g(θ) > 0 for all θ ∈ [a, b], then U is contained in the set Oα0 = {(r, θ) | r > 0, α0 < θ < α 0 + 2π} , and Φ(U) = D. Applying the change of variables theorem to the mapping Φ : Oα0 → R2 gives the desired formula (6.11) immediately.Chapter 6.
